section.title: How Long, O Lord
section.sectionRationale: One of the shortest and most tightly patterned laments in the Psalter, moving in six verses from a fourfold cry of 'how long' through urgent petition to a resolved vow of trust and song. It is a model of how honest complaint and settled faith can live in the very same prayer, and it stands early in Book I among David's psalms of personal distress.
section.thesis: David's raw, repeated 'how long' does not stay in despair; brought honestly before God it turns, without any change in circumstance, into trust in His loving devotion and a resolve to sing.
section.frameNote: Three short movements: the fourfold complaint, how long; the urgent petition, consider and answer me; and the turn to trust, I have trusted, I will sing.

verses.text:
How long, O LORD? Will You forget me forever? 
How long will You hide Your face from me?

How long must I wrestle in my {{nephesh|soul}}, with sorrow in my heart each day? 
How long will my enemy dominate me?
Consider me and respond, O LORD my God. Give light to my eyes, lest I sleep in death,
lest my enemy say, “I have overcome him,” and my foes rejoice when I fall.

units.label: I will sing to the Lord

verses.text: But I have trusted in Your {{chesed|loving devotion}}; my heart will rejoice in Your {{yeshuah|salvation}}.
I will sing to the LORD, for He has been good to me.

nephesh.gloss: Soul, inner self, the whole living person including mind and emotion, not merely a detached spirit. David wrestles in his nephesh, meaning the sorrow has settled into the very core of who he is, not just his outward circumstances.

chesed.gloss: Steadfast, covenant loving devotion, God's loyal love bound to His promises. It is the anchor David reaches for when nothing in his circumstances has changed; the feeling has not lifted, but the chesed he trusts in has not moved.

yeshuah.gloss: Salvation, deliverance, rescue. The same root behind the name Jesus, Yeshua, He saves. David's rejoicing in God's yeshuah before any rescue has arrived anticipates the fuller salvation later revealed and named in Christ.

basic.context: David feels forgotten and hidden from, worn down by sorrow every day, and pressed by an enemy who seems to be winning. He does not hide this from God; he brings it to Him directly, four times crying 'how long'. Then, still without any change in his circumstances, he asks God to look at him, to answer, to give him life. By the last two verses something has shifted inside him: he says he has trusted, and he resolves to sing.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a real prayer of lament, meant to be prayed, not merely studied. It gives permission and a pattern for honest complaint to God, and it shows that biblical faith does not require pretending things are fine; it requires bringing the real ache to the true God and choosing, even in the ache, to trust Him.
basic.originalLanguages: David wrestles in his nephesh, his whole inner self, not merely at the surface. What steadies him is not new information but chesed, God's steadfast covenant love, unchanged by David's feelings. His hope is called yeshuah, salvation, the very word behind the name of Jesus, He saves.
basic.typology: David's pattern of honest lament turning to trust anticipates the greater sufferer, Christ, who also cried out in anguish, 'My God, my God, why have You forsaken me', and who, like David here, entrusted Himself finally to the Father who saves.
basic.christ: Jesus prayed His own 'how long' from the cross, quoting a psalm just a few pages later, Psalm 22. He shows us that crying out to God in raw distress is not unbelief; it is the shape faith takes under real suffering, a faith that, like David's, still ends in trust and praise.
basic.god: God is not offended by David's repeated 'how long'. He is the kind of God who can be pressed, questioned, and pleaded with, and who is trusted precisely because His loving devotion, His chesed, does not depend on how quickly He answers.
basic.crossReferences: Psalm 22:1-2 voices the same 'how long' cry, later prayed by Christ on the cross. Romans 8:24-25 speaks of a hope that waits patiently for what is not yet seen. Lamentations 3:19-24 moves through the same pattern, from anguish to remembered steadfast love.
basic.oneStory: David's short prayer shows the whole Bible's pattern in miniature: honest cries in a broken world met by the steadfast love of God, a love fully proven in Christ, who endured His own greater 'how long' so that our songs of trust would have a sure and final foundation.

context.literary: A tightly compressed individual lament, its six verses moving through three clear stages, fourfold complaint, urgent petition, and resolved trust, a shape repeated across many of Israel's laments and often called the movement 'from plea to praise'.
context.historical: Attributed to David, likely from a season of prolonged personal distress and pursuit by an enemy, though no specific event is named, allowing every generation of sufferers since to pray it as their own.
context.canonical: One of the clearest short models in the Psalter of lament as an act of faith rather than a failure of it, a pattern that runs from Job's complaints through the prophets' laments to Gethsemane and the cross.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To give voice to unresolved suffering honestly before God, and to show that such honesty, far from opposing faith, is the very path by which trust is renewed.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes David's specific experience of feeling forgotten, but it functions prescriptively as a pattern for prayer: bring the ache directly to God, ask boldly, and choose to trust and sing even before circumstances change.

debates.question: Does David's shift to trust in verses 5-6 mean his circumstances had already changed?

views.label: Nothing in the text changes; the shift is internal, an act of will and faith
views.case: No deliverance is narrated between verse 4 and verse 5; the turn happens purely through prayer itself, showing that trust can precede rescue.

views.label: Some form of assurance, perhaps a word from a priest or an inward conviction, prompted the change
views.case: Similar shifts elsewhere in the Psalter, for example Psalm 6:8-9, may reflect an oracle of assurance received in worship, though the text does not say so explicitly here.

debates.bearing: Either reading affirms that the turn to trust is genuine and Spirit-given; the psalm still teaches that faith can rest in God's character before it sees His deliverance.

study.originalLanguages: David's distress reaches into his nephesh, his whole inner life, and his relief comes not from a change in circumstance but from resting again in God's chesed, His covenant loving devotion, and from anticipating His yeshuah, salvation, the word later given as the name of Jesus.

typology.type: David's unanswered 'how long' cried out in faith
typology.fulfillment: Christ's own cry of abandonment on the cross, Matthew 27:46, quoting Psalm 22, showing the true sufferer who bears the fullest 'how long' for us.

typology.type: David's trust in God's yeshuah before deliverance is seen
typology.fulfillment: The salvation named and accomplished in Jesus, Yeshua, whose resurrection proves that trust placed before rescue is not misplaced.

study.christ: David asks how long God will hide His face, and centuries later Jesus experiences the deepest form of that hiding on the cross, quoting Psalm 22's version of this same cry. David's resolve to trust in God's chesed and rejoice in His yeshuah anticipates the greater salvation secured once for all in Christ's death and resurrection, so that every believer's 'how long' now has a certain answer.
study.god: God permits and even invites this kind of raw questioning; He is not shown as distant or offended, but as the covenant Lord whose chesed remains steady even while His face feels hidden. The psalm reveals a God who can bear our complaint and who is faithful enough to be trusted before the rescue arrives.

crossReferences.note: The same cry of divine absence, later prayed word for word by Christ from the cross.
Moves from anguished memory of affliction to renewed hope in God's steadfast love, mirroring this psalm's turn.
Describes hope that waits patiently for what is not yet seen, the same posture David models here.

study.oneStory: In six verses David shows the whole shape of biblical hope: honest complaint brought to God, urgent petition, and a trust that sings before the answer comes. That trust finds its full ground in Christ, who bore the deepest 'how long' on the cross so that every sufferer's cry could end, like David's, in confident song.

quiz.q: What is the pattern of Psalm 13's structure?

options.text: Fourfold complaint, urgent petition, then a resolved turn to trust and song.
options.note: This three part movement, from plea to praise, is the psalm's clear shape.

options.text: A long complaint followed by a description of the enemy's defeat.
options.note: Close, but no defeat of the enemy is narrated; the turn is internal trust, not a reported victory.

options.text: A song of praise interrupted by a brief complaint.
options.note: This reverses the actual order; the complaint comes first and the praise follows.

options.text: A series of unrelated proverbs about patience.
options.note: The psalm is a unified personal lament and prayer, not a collection of proverbs.

quiz.q: What does David ask for in verses 3-4?

options.text: That God would consider him, answer him, and give light to his eyes so he would not sleep in death.
options.note: This is his direct, urgent petition for God's attention and life.

options.text: That God would destroy his enemy immediately.
options.note: David mentions the enemy's gloating as a concern, but his direct request is for God's own attention and life, not the enemy's destruction.

options.text: That God would explain why He has hidden His face.
options.note: Close to his complaint in verses 1-2, but his actual petition in verses 3-4 asks for God's attention and life, not an explanation.

options.text: That his sorrow would simply be forgotten.
options.note: David does not ask to forget his sorrow; he asks God to act and answer him.

quiz.q: What has changed by verses 5-6, and what has not?

options.text: No circumstance is shown to change, yet David's heart moves to trust in God's loving devotion and resolves to sing.
options.note: The text narrates no rescue; the shift is a fresh act of trust and praise.

options.text: David's enemy is explicitly defeated before he sings.
options.note: No defeat of the enemy is narrated in the text.

options.text: David receives a direct audible answer from God before rejoicing.
options.note: No such audible answer is recorded; his trust rests on God's character, not a reported new revelation.

options.text: His sorrow is instantly removed and forgotten.
options.note: His posture changes to trust and song, though this reflects a shift in his heart more than an erasure of the underlying sorrow.

quiz.q: What does the Hebrew word chesed emphasize in verse 5?

options.text: God's steadfast, covenant loving devotion, loyal love bound to His promises rather than to circumstances.
options.note: Chesed is the anchor David reaches for precisely because it does not depend on his feelings or timing.

options.text: A general feeling of niceness or pleasantness.
options.note: Chesed carries far more weight than a vague pleasant feeling; it is covenant loyalty and loving commitment.

options.text: God's raw power to defeat enemies.
options.note: Chesed refers to loving devotion and loyalty, not primarily to displays of power.

options.text: A momentary emotion that quickly fades.
options.note: This misses chesed's core meaning as a steadfast, enduring commitment rather than a passing emotion.

quiz.q: How does this psalm anticipate Christ?

options.text: Jesus later prays a parallel 'how long' cry from the cross, quoting Psalm 22, and models the same pattern of honest anguish resolved in trust toward the Father.
options.note: Christ's own cry of abandonment shows the same honest lament, ultimately entrusted to the Father who saves.

options.text: It predicts the exact day and hour of the crucifixion.
options.note: The psalm gives no such specific prediction; its connection to Christ is in the pattern of lament and trust, not a date.

options.text: It describes David's own future resurrection.
options.note: The psalm is about David's present distress and trust, not a stated hope of his own bodily resurrection.

options.text: It has no meaningful connection to the New Testament.
options.note: This understates the strong pattern connection, especially to Christ's cry of dereliction on the cross.

questions: David cries 'how long' four times without pretending things are fine. Where do you need to bring an honest, unresolved 'how long' to God this week rather than hiding it?
David asks God to 'consider me and respond' before anything has changed. What would it look like for you to pray boldly for God's attention even while your circumstances remain the same?

By the end of the psalm, David trusts and resolves to sing though nothing outward has shifted. Have you ever experienced that kind of inward turn to trust before the answer came? What helped it happen?
Chesed, God's steadfast loving devotion, does not depend on David's feelings. Where are you tempted to measure God's love by your circumstances rather than by His unchanging character?
Jesus prayed His own 'how long' from the cross. How does knowing that Christ entered fully into this kind of honest anguish change the way you bring your own laments to God?

translationNotes.note: The fourfold repetition of 'how long' in the Hebrew intensifies the sense of prolonged, unresolved distress; English translations preserve this by repeating the phrase at the start of each clause.
Loving devotion renders chesed, God's covenant loyal love; some translations render it lovingkindness or steadfast love, all pointing to the same durable, promise-keeping love.
Salvation renders yeshuah, the same root later given as the personal name Jesus, Yeshua, He saves, linking David's hope of rescue to the name of the one who fulfills it.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Psalm 13:1-6. No em or en dashes present in the source text or in this file.
